content.text: Beyond Files and Shell: System-Level Tools

A complete agent toolkit for software engineering tasks needs tools that reach further than file read/write and shell commands: process management (start a server, check if a test runner is still running), environment inspection (which Python is on PATH, what port is in use), network operations (download a file, check an endpoint is live), and system health reads (how much RAM is left, is the disk full). Each category has different risk profiles and requires different safety controls.

attrs.alt: Four system tool categories: process tools, environment tools, network tools, system info — each with safety boundaries
attrs.title: System tool categories for AI agents

content.text: Process Management Tools
Agents that manage running services — starting a dev server, running tests in the background, stopping a hung process — need process tools. The
psutil
library exposes cross-platform process information without requiring shell access.
Environment and Path Tools

Agents debugging build failures or configuring tool chains need to inspect the shell environment: which Python binary is active, whether a required environment variable is set, which port a service is bound to. These are read-mostly operations with one critical safety rule: never expose secret-looking variables to the model.
Network Tools
Agents running integration tests or deployment workflows need to make HTTP requests, download files, and verify that services are reachable. Wrap
httpx
(sync or async) rather than exposing curl via the shell tool. This keeps output structured and enforces timeouts and size limits programmatically.

import httpx, ipaddress, urllib.parse
from pathlib import Path

# Domains the agent is allowed to reach
ALLOWED_DOMAINS: set[str] | None = None  # None = allow all (restrict in production)
MAX_RESPONSE_BYTES = 1_048_576  # 1 MB

def _check_url_safety(url: str) -> None:
    """Reject URLs that target private IP ranges or disallowed domains."""
    parsed = urllib.parse.urlparse(url)
    host = parsed.hostname or ""
    if ALLOWED_DOMAINS is not None and host not in ALLOWED_DOMAINS:
        raise ValueError(f"Domain '{host}' is not on the allowed list.")
    # Block RFC-1918 private ranges
    try:
        addr = ipaddress.ip_address(host)
        if addr.is_private or addr.is_loopback:
            raise ValueError(f"Requests to private/loopback address {host} are not allowed.")
    except ValueError as e:
        if "not allowed" in str(e):
            raise
        pass  # hostname, not IP — let DNS resolve it

def http_get(url: str, headers: dict | None = None, timeout: float = 15.0) -> dict:
    """Make an HTTP GET request and return status + body (truncated to 1MB)."""
    _check_url_safety(url)
    with httpx.Client(timeout=timeout, follow_redirects=True) as client:
        response = client.get(url, headers=headers or {})
    body = response.text[:MAX_RESPONSE_BYTES]
    return {
        "url": str(response.url),
        "status_code": response.status_code,
        "headers": dict(response.headers),
        "body": body,
        "truncated": len(response.content) > MAX_RESPONSE_BYTES,
    }

def http_post(url: str, json_body: dict | None = None, headers: dict | None = None, timeout: float = 15.0) -> dict:
    """Make an HTTP POST request with a JSON body."""
    _check_url_safety(url)
    with httpx.Client(timeout=timeout) as client:
        response = client.post(url, json=json_body, headers=headers or {})
    return {
        "url": str(response.url),
        "status_code": response.status_code,
        "body": response.text[:MAX_RESPONSE_BYTES],
    }

def download_file(url: str, dest_path: str, max_bytes: int = 50_000_000) -> dict:
    """Download a file from URL and save it to the workspace.

    dest_path must be inside the workspace root (validated via validate_path).
    """
    from file_tools import validate_path  # reuse path validation from file tools
    resolved = validate_path(dest_path, must_exist=False)
    _check_url_safety(url)
    with httpx.stream("GET", url, timeout=60, follow_redirects=True) as response:
        response.raise_for_status()
        written = 0
        with resolved.open("wb") as f:
            for chunk in response.iter_bytes(chunk_size=65536):
                f.write(chunk)
                written += len(chunk)
                if written > max_bytes:
                    raise ValueError(f"Download exceeded {max_bytes} byte limit.")
    return {"url": url, "dest": str(resolved), "bytes_written": written}
System Information Tools

Read-only system metrics let agents make informed decisions: skip heavy compilation if memory is low, warn before filling a disk partition, or detect which OS the agent is running on to choose the right command syntax.
Assembling the Full System Toolkit
Register all four categories as Claude tools. Group related tools in the description so the model understands what is available without having to infer from tool names alone.
Summary

System tools extend an agent beyond file and shell operations into process lifecycle management, environment inspection, network access, and system health monitoring. Each category has a distinct safety profile: process tools must refuse to kill system PIDs and restrict to agent-owned processes; environment tools must redact secret variables; network tools should enforce an allowed-domain list and block private IP ranges; system info tools are read-only and low risk. Implementing these as typed Python functions wrapped in Anthropic tool schemas gives the model a clean, safe interface to the underlying OS.
